title: Foot rot treatment in sheep
subtitle: Identifying, trimming, and treating the most common lameness in a small flock.
excerpt: Foot rot is the leading cause of lameness in UK sheep and spreads rapidly between animals on wet pasture. This covers the difference between scald and true foot rot, the trimming and footbath protocol, and when the problem needs antibiotic treatment rather than footbathing alone.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code for Sheep (2023). AHDB Beef and Lamb, lameness five-point plan. APHA Scald and Foot Rot Advisory Note.

content.text: Lameness in a small flock on wet spring or autumn pasture is almost always

. Distinguishing between them matters because scald responds to footbathing alone, while true foot rot requires antibiotic injection alongside footbath treatment. A good diagnostic trim at the start saves wasted footbathing time.

Use a shepherd's crook to catch the lame animal from the flock. Sit it on its rump with its back against your legs. With the animal stable, examine the foot before trimming: smell the hoof (foot rot produces a distinctive sweet-rotten smell that scald does not), look at the interdigital cleft for inflammation and swelling, and note whether the animal is bearing weight on the foot or holding it off the ground entirely.

to pare back excess horn and reveal the internal hoof tissue. Trim until the white sole is visible. If the tissue underneath is pink and intact, this is scald: treat with the footbath. If you see pale grey, soft, crumbling tissue with a strong smell when the horn is opened, this is
and indicates true foot rot. Antibiotic injection is needed from the vet.

A 10% zinc sulphate solution in the footbath trough is the standard UK option: less corrosive than formalin, effective against both scald and as a secondary treatment for foot rot. Stand each animal in the trough for 30 to 60 seconds. Run the whole flock through every five to seven days for three to four consecutive treatments. Change the solution when it becomes heavily contaminated.

attrs.body: Aggressive trimming that exposes the sensitive laminae makes the foot more painful and more vulnerable to infection. Trim until the horn is level and the sole is visible; stop there. The aim is to remove the overgrown and diseased material, not to expose healthy tissue.
attrs.title: Do not over-trim
